svg.text: System Structures What Operating Systems Do

frame.data-narration: A monolithic operating system is a type of operating system where the kernel and the applications are combined into a single executable file. The kernel and applications are tightly coupled, and the operating system is a self-contained unit. It is difficult to modify or extend the operating system. MS-DOS is an example of a monolithic operating system (old version).

A microkernel operating system is a type of operating system where the kernel is minimal and the applications are separate from the kernel. The kernel provides basic services such as process scheduling and memory management. Linux kernel version 1.x is an example of a microkernel operating system.

A hybrid operating system is a type of operating system that combines elements of both monolithic and microkernel operating systems. The kernel provides basic services such as process scheduling and memory management, and the applications are separate from the kernel. Windows NT kernel version 3.x is an example of a hybrid operating system.

path.data-label: Combines elements of monolithic and microkernel operating systems

frame.data-narration: A real-time operating system is a type of operating system that ensures predictability and reliability in real-time systems. It provides guarantees about the timing and behavior of tasks and processes.

path.data-label: Ensures predictability and reliability in real-time systems
Combines elements of monolithic and microkernel operating systems
Ensures predictability and reliability in real-time systems
Ensures predictability and reliability in real-time systems

Ensures predictability and reliability in real-time systems
